title: Die drei Sprachen
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 33. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The thirty-third tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 33), the story of a count’s ‘dull’ son who learns, over three years with three masters, only the languages of dogs, of birds, and of frogs. Cast out to be killed and spared by pitying servants, he finds each animal tongue turns providential: the dogs’ speech wins him a guarded treasure, and the frogs’ croaking foretells that he will be made Pope — which comes to pass when two snow-white doves settle on his shoulders at the papal election. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as ward, mußt, thun, Thurm, Thiere, Pabst, tödten, quacken and unschlüßig.

sentences.t: In der Schweiz lebte einmal ein alter Graf, der hatte nur einen einzigen Sohn, aber er war dumm und konnte nichts lernen.
sentences.l: In Switzerland there once lived an old count, who had only a single son, but he was dull and could learn nothing.
sentences.i: In Switzerland there once lived an old count who had but a single son; the boy, however, was dull and could learn nothing.
sentences.n: “der hatte” — German resumes a noun with a demonstrative der/die/das (‘who had’), a construction you meet constantly in Grimm. “dumm” here is ‘dull, slow-witted,’ not ‘stupid’ in a cutting sense.

sentences.t: Da sprach der Vater „höre, mein Sohn, ich bringe nichts in deinen Kopf, ich mag es anfangen wie ich will.
sentences.l: Then spoke the father, “hear, my son, I bring nothing into your head, I may set about it as I will.
sentences.i: Then the father said, “Listen, my son, I can get nothing into your head, however I go about it.
sentences.n: “ich mag es anfangen wie ich will” — here mögen keeps its older sense ‘however I may (try), no matter how I set about it.’ “anfangen” (to begin, set about) is separable, but stays together after the modal.

sentences.t: Du mußt fort von hier, ich will dich einem berühmten Meister übergeben, der soll es mit dir versuchen.“
sentences.l: You must away from here, I will hand you over to a famous master, he shall try it with you.”
sentences.i: You must leave here; I will hand you over to a famous master, and he shall try what he can do with you.”
sentences.n: “mußt” is the 1857 spelling of musst. “fort von hier” — fort (away) with the verb of motion dropped: ‘[go] away from here.’ “übergeben” takes the dative: hand over *to* a master (einem Meister).

sentences.t: Der Junge ward in eine fremde Stadt geschickt, und blieb bei dem Meister ein ganzes Jahr.
sentences.l: The boy was sent into a strange town, and stayed with the master a whole year.
sentences.i: The boy was sent to a strange town and stayed a whole year with the master.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘was, became’), here forming the passive ‘was sent’; modern German uses wurde. You will meet ward throughout.

sentences.t: Nach Verlauf dieser Zeit kam er wieder heim, und der Vater fragte „nun, mein Sohn, was hast du gelernt?“
sentences.l: After the passing of this time he came home again, and the father asked, “now, my son, what have you learned?”
sentences.i: When this time had passed he came home again, and the father asked, “Now, my son, what have you learned?”
sentences.n: “nach Verlauf dieser Zeit” = after the lapse of this time. “kam ... heim” (heimkommen, to come home) is separable.

sentences.t: „Vater, ich habe gelernt was die Hunde bellen“ antwortete er.
sentences.l: “Father, I have learned what the dogs bark,” he answered.
sentences.n: The tale’s central marvel: the boy learns the speech of animals. “was die Hunde bellen” = ‘what the dogs bark [i.e. say].’ This gift, which looks worthless, will three times prove providential.

sentences.t: „Daß Gott erbarm,“ rief der Vater aus, „ist das alles, was du gelernt hast? ich will dich in eine andere Stadt zu einem andern Meister thun.“
sentences.l: “That God have mercy,” cried the father out, “is that all that you have learned? I will put you into another town to another master.”
sentences.i: “God have mercy!” the father cried, “is that all you have learned? I will send you to another town, to another master.”
sentences.n: “Daß Gott erbarm” is a fixed exclamation, ‘God have mercy!’ — erbarm is a shortened old subjunctive (‘may God have mercy’). “rief ... aus” (ausrufen, to cry out) is separable. “thun” is the 1857 spelling of tun; here ‘to put, place.’ “andern” is the old spelling of anderen.

sentences.t: Der Junge ward hingebracht, und blieb bei diesem Meister auch ein Jahr.
sentences.l: The boy was brought there, and stayed with this master too a year.
sentences.i: The boy was taken there, and stayed a year with this master as well.
sentences.n: “hingebracht” (hinbringen, to take/bring there) is separable; hin- marks motion away toward a place. “ward” again forms the passive.

sentences.t: Als er zurückkam, fragte der Vater wiederum „mein Sohn, was hast du gelernt?“
sentences.l: When he came back, the father asked once more, “my son, what have you learned?”
sentences.n: “zurückkam” (zurückkommen, to come back) is separable, here fused inside the als-clause. “wiederum” = once again, in turn.

sentences.t: Er antwortete „Vater, ich habe gelernt was die Vögli sprechen.“
sentences.l: He answered, “Father, I have learned what the little-birds speak.”
sentences.i: He answered, “Father, I have learned what the birds say.”
sentences.n: “Vögli” is a Swiss-German diminutive of Vögel (‘little birds’) — the tale is set in Switzerland and keeps a dialect touch. The second language: the speech of birds.

sentences.t: Da gerieth der Vater in Zorn und sprach „o du verlorner Mensch, hast die kostbare Zeit hingebracht und nichts gelernt, und schämst dich nicht mir unter die Augen zu treten?
sentences.l: Then the father fell into wrath and spoke, “o you lost creature, you have spent the precious time and learned nothing, and are you not ashamed to step before my eyes?
sentences.i: Then the father flew into a rage and said, “Oh, you good-for-nothing, you have wasted the precious time and learned nothing, and are you not ashamed to come before my eyes?
sentences.n: “gerieth in Zorn” — geraten (1857 gerieth) means ‘to fall into (a state)’; so ‘to fly into a rage.’ “verlorner Mensch” = a ‘lost’ person, a good-for-nothing. “die Zeit hinbringen” = to pass/spend the time. “jemandem unter die Augen treten” = to come before someone’s eyes, to face them. “schämst dich” — sich schämen, to be ashamed (reflexive).

sentences.t: Ich will dich zu einem dritten Meister schicken, aber lernst du auch diesmal nichts, so will ich dein Vater nicht mehr sein.“
sentences.l: I will send you to a third master, but learn you also this time nothing, then I will be your father no more.”
sentences.i: I will send you to a third master; but if this time too you learn nothing, then I will no longer be your father.”
sentences.n: “lernst du ... nichts, so ...” — a conditional made by putting the verb first (‘if you learn nothing, then ...’), with no wenn. “diesmal” = this time.

sentences.t: Der Sohn blieb bei dem dritten Meister ebenfalls ein ganzes Jahr, und als er wieder nach Haus kam und der Vater fragte „mein Sohn, was hast du gelernt?“ so antwortete er „lieber Vater, ich habe dieses Jahr gelernt was die Frösche quacken.“
sentences.l: The son stayed with the third master likewise a whole year, and when he came home again and the father asked, “my son, what have you learned?” then he answered, “dear father, this year I have learned what the frogs croak.”
sentences.i: The son stayed a whole year with the third master as well; and when he came home again and the father asked, “My son, what have you learned?” he answered, “Dear father, this year I have learned what the frogs croak.”
sentences.n: “ebenfalls” = likewise, as well. “quacken” is the 1857 spelling of quaken, ‘to croak.’ The third language: the speech of frogs — the one that will foretell his destiny.

sentences.t: Da gerieth der Vater in den höchsten Zorn, sprang auf, rief seine Leute herbei und sprach „dieser Mensch ist mein Sohn nicht mehr, ich stoße ihn aus und gebiete euch daß ihr ihn hinaus in den Wald führt und ihm das Leben nehmt.“
sentences.l: Then the father fell into the highest wrath, sprang up, called his people hither and spoke, “this man is my son no more, I cast him out and command you that you lead him out into the forest and take his life from him.”
sentences.i: Then the father flew into the greatest rage, sprang up, called his people to him, and said, “This man is my son no longer; I cast him out, and command you to lead him out into the forest and take his life.”
sentences.n: “sprang auf” (aufspringen) and “rief ... herbei” (herbeirufen) are separable. “stoße ... aus” (ausstoßen, to cast out, disown) is separable too. “ihm das Leben nehmen” — a dative of the person: ‘take the life from him.’ “gebiete” (gebieten, to command) governs the daß-clause that follows.

sentences.t: Sie führten ihn hinaus, aber als sie ihn tödten sollten, konnten sie nicht vor Mitleiden und ließen ihn gehen.
sentences.l: They led him out, but when they were to kill him, they could not, for pity, and let him go.
sentences.i: They led him out, but when they were to kill him, they could not do it out of pity, and let him go.
sentences.n: “tödten” is the 1857 spelling of töten (‘to kill’), with the old -dt-. “konnten sie nicht vor Mitleiden” — the verb of the deed is left understood: ‘they could not [do it] for pity.’ “vor” + dative here gives cause: ‘out of, for.’

sentences.t: Sie schnitten einem Reh Augen und Zunge aus, damit sie dem Alten die Wahrzeichen bringen konnten.
sentences.l: They cut out of a roe-deer eyes and tongue, so that they could bring the old man the tokens.
sentences.i: They cut out the eyes and tongue of a roe-deer, so that they could bring the old man the proofs.
sentences.n: “schnitten ... aus” (ausschneiden, to cut out) is separable. “einem Reh” is a dative of possession: ‘cut out of a roe-deer [its] eyes and tongue.’ “Wahrzeichen” = tokens, proofs — the false evidence of the killing, a folktale commonplace (compare the huntsman in Snow White).

sentences.t: Der Jüngling wanderte fort und kam nach einiger Zeit zu einer Burg, wo er um Nachtherberge bat.
sentences.l: The youth wandered forth and came after some time to a castle, where he asked for a night’s lodging.
sentences.i: The young man wandered on, and after some time came to a castle, where he asked for a night’s lodging.
sentences.n: “wanderte fort” (fortwandern, to wander off) is separable. “um etwas bitten” = to ask for something. “Nachtherberge” = shelter for the night, a night’s lodging.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ sagte der Burgherr, „wenn du da unten in dem alten Thurm übernachten willst, so gehe hin, aber ich warne dich, es ist lebensgefährlich, denn er ist voll wilder Hunde, die bellen und heulen in einem fort, und zu gewissen Stunden müssen sie einen Menschen ausgeliefert haben, den sie auch gleich verzehren.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” said the lord of the castle, “if you want to spend the night down there in the old tower, then go there, but I warn you, it is deadly dangerous, for it is full of wild dogs that bark and howl on and on, and at certain hours they must have a man delivered up to them, whom they also devour at once.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” said the lord of the castle, “if you will spend the night down there in the old tower, then go; but I warn you, it is deadly dangerous, for it is full of wild dogs that bark and howl without cease, and at certain hours they must be given a man, whom they devour on the spot.”
sentences.n: “Thurm” carries the old -th- (modern Turm). “in einem fort” is an idiom, ‘without stopping, on and on.’ “lebensgefährlich” = life-threatening, deadly dangerous. “einen Menschen ausgeliefert haben” — ausliefern, to hand over, deliver up.

sentences.t: Die ganze Gegend war darüber in Trauer und Leid, und konnte doch niemand helfen.
sentences.l: The whole region was over it in mourning and sorrow, and yet no one could help.
sentences.i: The whole region was in mourning and grief over it, and yet no one could help.
sentences.n: “darüber” = ‘over it, about it.’ “Trauer und Leid” is a stock pairing, ‘mourning and sorrow.’ Note the subject niemand standing after the verb: ‘and yet could no one help.’

sentences.t: Der Jüngling aber war ohne Furcht und sprach „laßt mich nur hinab zu den bellenden Hunden, und gebt mir etwas, das ich ihnen vorwerfen kann; mir sollen sie nichts thun.“
sentences.l: The youth, however, was without fear and spoke, “just let me down to the barking dogs, and give me something that I can throw to them; to me they shall do nothing.”
sentences.i: But the young man was without fear and said, “Only let me down to the barking dogs, and give me something to throw them; they shall do me no harm.”
sentences.n: “laßt” and “gebt” are plural imperatives. “hinab” = down (away from the speaker); the verb of motion is dropped after laßt. “vorwerfen” (to throw before, throw to) is separable. “mir sollen sie nichts thun” — the dative mir is fronted for emphasis, ‘to me they shall do nothing.’

sentences.t: Weil er nun selber nicht anders wollte, so gaben sie ihm etwas Essen für die wilden Thiere und brachten ihn hinab zu dem Thurm.
sentences.l: Because he now himself wanted it no otherwise, they gave him some food for the wild beasts and brought him down to the tower.
sentences.i: Since he himself would have it no other way, they gave him some food for the wild beasts and took him down to the tower.
sentences.n: The old resumptive “weil ..., so ...” (‘because ..., [then] ...’); modern German drops the so. “Thiere” is the 1857 spelling of Tiere (‘beasts’), with the old -th-. “nicht anders wollen” = to want it no other way.

sentences.t: Als er hinein trat, bellten ihn die Hunde nicht an, wedelten mit den Schwänzen ganz freundlich um ihn herum, fraßen was er ihnen hinsetzte und krümmten ihm kein Härchen.
sentences.l: When he stepped in, the dogs did not bark at him, wagged with their tails quite friendlily around him, ate what he set before them and bent him not a little hair.
sentences.i: When he stepped in, the dogs did not bark at him, but wagged their tails quite amiably about him, ate what he set down for them, and did not harm a hair of his.
sentences.n: “bellten ... an” (anbellen, to bark at) is separable, split around the negation. “fraßen” — fressen is the verb for an animal’s (greedy) eating, not essen. “jemandem kein Härchen krümmen” is an idiom, ‘not to bend a single little hair of someone,’ i.e. to do them no harm at all. The first language pays off: the dogs’ speech has become friendship.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen kam er zu jedermanns Erstaunen gesund und unversehrt wieder zum Vorschein und sagte zu dem Burgherrn „die Hunde haben mir in ihrer Sprache offenbart warum sie da hausen und dem Lande Schaden bringen.
sentences.l: On the next morning he came, to everyone’s astonishment, healthy and unharmed again to view, and said to the lord of the castle, “the dogs have revealed to me in their language why they dwell there and bring harm to the land.
sentences.i: The next morning, to everyone’s astonishment, he came to light again safe and sound, and said to the lord of the castle, “In their own tongue the dogs have revealed to me why they haunt this place and do the land harm.
sentences.n: “zum Vorschein kommen” is an idiom, ‘to come to light, appear.’ “andern” = anderen (‘next’). “hausen” = to dwell, but with a menacing sense, ‘to haunt, wreak havoc.’ “dem Lande” has the old dative -e. Again the animal-speech gift drives the plot.

sentences.t: Sie sind verwünscht und müssen einen großen Schatz hüten, der unten im Thurme liegt und kommen nicht eher zur Ruhe als bis er gehoben ist, und wie dies geschehen muß, das habe ich ebenfalls aus ihren Reden vernommen.“
sentences.l: They are bewitched and must guard a great treasure that lies below in the tower, and come not to rest until it is raised, and how this must happen, that I have likewise gathered from their talk.”
sentences.i: They are under a curse and must guard a great treasure that lies below in the tower, and they find no rest until it is lifted; and how this must be done, that too I have learned from their talk.”
sentences.n: “verwünscht” = cursed, put under a spell. “einen Schatz heben” = to raise/dig up a buried treasure — the fixed verb for lifting hidden treasure. “nicht eher ... als bis” = ‘not before ... until,’ i.e. not until. “Thurme” has the old dative -e. “vernommen” (vernehmen) = to perceive, learn, gather.

sentences.t: Da freuten sich alle die das hörten, und der Burgherr sagte er wollte ihn an Sohnes statt annehmen, wenn er es glücklich vollbrächte.
sentences.l: Then all who heard that rejoiced, and the lord of the castle said he would take him on in a son’s stead, if he brought it happily to pass.
sentences.i: Then all who heard it rejoiced, and the lord of the castle said he would take him in place of a son, if he carried it off successfully.
sentences.n: “sich freuen” = to rejoice (reflexive). “an Sohnes statt” = in the stead of a son (an old genitive with statt, ‘instead of’) — to adopt him as a son. “vollbrächte” is subjunctive II of vollbringen (‘to accomplish’), marking both the condition and the reported promise. “wollte” here is reported ‘he said he would.’

sentences.t: Er stieg wieder hinab, und weil er wußte was er zu thun hatte, so vollführte er es und brachte eine mit Gold gefüllte Truhe herauf.
sentences.l: He climbed down again, and because he knew what he had to do, he carried it out and brought up a chest filled with gold.
sentences.i: He climbed down again, and since he knew what he had to do, he accomplished it and brought up a chest filled with gold.
sentences.n: “stieg ... hinab” (hinabsteigen) and “brachte ... herauf” (heraufbringen) are separable. “wußte” and “thun” are the 1857 spellings of wusste and tun. “vollführen” = to carry out, execute. “eine mit Gold gefüllte Truhe” — an attributive participle phrase, ‘a with-gold-filled chest.’

sentences.t: Das Geheul der wilden Hunde ward von nun an nicht mehr gehört, sie waren verschwunden, und das Land war von der Plage befreit.
sentences.l: The howling of the wild dogs was from now on heard no more, they had vanished, and the land was freed from the plague.
sentences.i: The howling of the wild dogs was heard no more from that time on; they had vanished, and the land was freed from the plague.
sentences.n: “Geheul” — the ge-...-l pattern forms a collective noun of continuous sound, ‘a howling all over’ (compare Gebell, Gerede). “von nun an” = from now on. “ward” once more forms the passive. “von der Plage befreit” — befreien von, to free from.

sentences.t: Über eine Zeit kam es ihm in den Sinn, er wollte nach Rom fahren.
sentences.l: After a while it came into his mind, he wanted to travel to Rome.
sentences.i: After a while it came into his mind that he would travel to Rome.
sentences.n: “über eine Zeit” = after a while, some time later. “es kommt jemandem in den Sinn” = it comes into someone’s mind, occurs to them (dative ihm).

sentences.t: Auf dem Weg kam er an einem Sumpf vorbei, in welchem Frösche saßen und quackten.
sentences.l: On the way he came past a marsh, in which frogs sat and croaked.
sentences.i: On the way he passed by a marsh in which frogs sat and croaked.
sentences.n: “kam ... vorbei” (vorbeikommen, to come past) is separable. “quackten” is the 1857 spelling of quakten (‘croaked’). “in welchem” = ‘in which,’ a relative a touch more formal than in dem.

sentences.t: Er horchte auf, und als er vernahm was sie sprachen, ward er ganz nachdenklich und traurig.
sentences.l: He pricked up his ears, and when he heard what they spoke, he grew quite thoughtful and sad.
sentences.i: He listened, and when he heard what they were saying, he grew quite pensive and sad.
sentences.n: “horchte auf” (aufhorchen, to prick up one’s ears, take notice) is separable. “vernahm” (vernehmen) = to hear, perceive. “ward” here = ‘became’ (grew). The frogs’ words trouble him — the reader is not yet told why.

sentences.t: Endlich langte er in Rom an, da war gerade der Pabst gestorben, und unter den Kardinälen großer Zweifel wen sie zum Nachfolger bestimmen sollten.
sentences.l: At last he arrived in Rome; there the Pope had just died, and among the cardinals great doubt whom they should appoint as successor.
sentences.i: At last he reached Rome, where the Pope had just died, and among the cardinals there was great doubt whom they should name as his successor.
sentences.n: “langte ... an” (anlangen, to arrive) is separable. “Pabst” is the 1857 spelling of Papst (‘Pope’). “wen” is the accusative of wer (‘whom’). Note the verbless clause ‘und ... großer Zweifel’ — ‘[there was] great doubt,’ with the ‘was’ understood.

sentences.t: Sie wurden zuletzt einig derjenige sollte zum Pabst erwählt werden, an dem sich ein göttliches Wunderzeichen offenbaren würde.
sentences.l: They became at last agreed: that one should be chosen as Pope on whom a divine sign of wonder would reveal itself.
sentences.i: In the end they agreed that he should be chosen Pope on whom a divine miracle-sign should show itself.
sentences.n: “einig werden” = to come to agreement. “derjenige ... an dem” = ‘the one ... on whom’ (a demonstrative paired with a relative). “sich offenbaren” = to reveal itself (reflexive). “würde” is subjunctive, marking the condition. “Wunderzeichen” = miraculous sign, portent — this sets up the omen.

sentences.t: Und als das eben beschlossen war, in demselben Augenblick trat der junge Graf in die Kirche, und plötzlich flogen zwei schneeweiße Tauben auf seine beiden Schultern und blieben da sitzen.
sentences.l: And when that had just been resolved, in the same moment the young count stepped into the church, and suddenly two snow-white doves flew onto his two shoulders and remained sitting there.
sentences.i: And just as that had been decided, at that very moment the young count stepped into the church, and suddenly two snow-white doves flew onto both his shoulders and stayed there.
sentences.n: “eben” = just (now). “beschlossen” (beschließen) = resolved, decided. “blieben da sitzen” = ‘remained sitting there’ (bleiben + bare infinitive). This is the papal-election omen: the dove as the sign of the Holy Spirit descending, here doubled and settling on the chosen man — the very thing the frogs foretold.

sentences.t: Die Geistlichkeit erkannte darin das Zeichen Gottes und fragte ihn auf der Stelle ob er Pabst werden wolle.
sentences.l: The clergy recognized therein the sign of God and asked him on the spot whether he would become Pope.
sentences.i: The clergy saw in it the sign of God, and asked him on the spot whether he would become Pope.
sentences.n: “Geistlichkeit” = the clergy, the assembled churchmen. “darin” = ‘therein, in it.’ “auf der Stelle” = on the spot, at once. “wolle” is subjunctive I of the reported question (‘whether he would’).

sentences.t: Er war unschlüßig und wußte nicht ob er dessen würdig wäre, aber die Tauben redeten ihm zu daß er es thun möchte, und endlich sagte er „ja.“
sentences.l: He was undecided and did not know whether he was worthy of it, but the doves talked to him that he should do it, and at last he said, “yes.”
sentences.i: He was irresolute and did not know whether he was worthy of it; but the doves urged him to do it, and at last he said, “Yes.”
sentences.n: “unschlüßig” is the 1857 spelling of unschlüssig (‘undecided’). “dessen würdig” = worthy of it (würdig takes the genitive dessen). “redeten ihm zu” (zureden, to urge, encourage) is separable and takes the dative. “wäre” and “möchte” are subjunctive of reported thought and wish.

sentences.t: Da wurde er gesalbt und geweiht, und damit war eingetroffen, was er von den Fröschen unterwegs gehört, und was ihn so bestürzt gemacht hatte, daß er der heilige Pabst werden sollte.
sentences.l: Then he was anointed and consecrated, and therewith had come to pass what he had heard from the frogs on the way, and what had so dismayed him — that he should become the holy Pope.
sentences.i: Then he was anointed and consecrated, and so what he had heard from the frogs on the way had come to pass, the very thing that had so dismayed him: that he should become the holy Pope.
sentences.n: “gesalbt und geweiht” = anointed and consecrated (the rite of ordination). “eingetroffen” (eintreffen, to come true, come to pass) is separable. “bestürzt” = dismayed, taken aback. Now the frogs’ prophecy is explained — this is what had made him so pensive and sad at the marsh.

sentences.t: Darauf mußte er eine Messe singen und wußte kein Wort davon, aber die zwei Tauben saßen stets auf seinen Schultern und sagten ihm alles ins Ohr.
sentences.l: Thereupon he had to sing a Mass and knew not a word of it, but the two doves sat ever on his shoulders and said everything to him into the ear.
sentences.i: After that he had to sing a Mass, and knew not a word of it; but the two doves sat ever on his shoulders and whispered everything into his ear.
sentences.n: “darauf” = thereupon, after that. “eine Messe singen” = to sing/celebrate a Mass. “stets” = ever, always. “sagten ihm ... ins Ohr” — a dative of the person, ‘said into his ear.’ The doves, once the omen, now become his help — as the animals’ speech has been throughout.
